entry.title: hhutil: A toolbox for retro handhelds

link.title: hhutil: A toolbox for retro handhelds

entry.content:
My newest obsession

Recently, I acquired a handheld console, one of those cheap ones, called the R36S. It’s been a few months, actually, but my excitement is so high it feels like I just turned it on for the first time yesterday. The experience reminds me of when I bought my NDS, which I still keep to this day. I could stay here talking about emulators and childhood games, but that’s not what got me excited; it was the memories of “hacking” my DS and installing every kind of Homebrew I could find on the classic GBATemp forums. It was like having a handheld computer before smartphones, and that’s exactly what these retro handhelds are: small ARM-based SBCs running a gaming-focused Linux. And I am completely obsessed.

There are several options for these devices on the market, at all prices and with all sorts of options. The one I have is a clone of a clone; the R36S is just the best-known member of a whole family of clones that basically share the same SoC, the Rockchip RK3326. And it’s a big family; there’s even an entire Wiki dedicated to documenting the different versions and variations appearing out there, as well as helping users install CFWs (Custom Firmware) on their devices. The community is extremely active, just look at the amount of posts made on Reddit every day.

And for creating games?

These consoles are quite limited, both in terms of hardware and software. According to the Wiki, the standard version of the R36S has the following specs:

Soc RockChip RK3326
CPU Quad-Core ARM Cortex-A35 (1.5 GHz)
GPU Mali-G31 MP2 (650 MHz)
RAM 1GB DDR3L
Display 3.5-inch IPS, 640x480
Wi-Fi External Dongle Only
Bluetooth Still no success

It’s no gaming PC. And in terms of software, it doesn’t seem like a big problem since it runs Linux and should run any language that can be compiled for ARM64. However, this GPU only supports OpenGL ES (which is not the same as OpenGL). Additionally, most of these CFWs do not include Wayland or X11, which makes the process even harder. Luckily, the PortMaster team has been making a great effort to improve compatibility for these devices, but the process is slow and much depends on changes within the engines themselves to work.

This is the case with my favorite engine, Ebitengine. Although in theory it should run without problems, as it supports OpenGL ES by default and Go can be easily compiled for ARM64, my experience wasn’t that simple. The program complained about not finding X11, and I didn’t have much luck with the PortMaster devs. Instead of continuing to bash my head against this problem, I preferred to switch to LÖVE and I’m quite satisfied with that choice. Not only is it a much more robust engine, but it’s also fully compatible with Portmaster without even needing compilation. Just zip the source code into a file with the .love extension and use the script available on their site. So that’s what I did; with some dedication and the help of great documentation, it was easy to do everything I wanted using LÖVE.

And what did I do?

So, hhutil is a collection of applications created specifically for retro consoles. Using a joystick as a controller and a resolution of only 640x480, these apps seek to deliver a native experience on these devices. I tried to create basic functionalities that make sense on a device like this and don’t require external accessories to function, in most cases. The repository with all applications can be found here, but here’s a brief presentation of each.

Calc

It’s a calculator with all the functions you expect and then some. In addition to the four basic operations, it also has trigonometric functions, logical operations, different number bases, and works with expressions. I also included a memory system with different variables, making this a quite powerful calculator, even more than some scientific ones on the market. Its algorithm gave me a lot of work and I believe there are still some bugs to be fixed in more complex cases, but overall I was happy with the result.

Dice

I wanted to make a dice rolling app that had animations but was at the same time minimalist and straight to the point. I think I found a good middle ground with this implementation. I used this 7-segment display aesthetic instead of spinning dice mainly because I wouldn’t need to create any art from scratch, but the result was better than expected. Along with the sound effects, I think it’s a good addition to any game night or TTRPG.

Metronome

I decided to take a risk by creating a metronome. In terms of design, I liked the result, but I confess my ears aren’t trained to tell if the logic is perfectly on point. I made sure to keep each button with only one function, so you don’t need to look at the screen while using it. I also added a variety of different sounds for variety; I particularly liked the result, and if my musical talents were sharper, I would definitely use this metronome to practice.

GPTester

This was my favorite one to create. It’s just an app to test the console buttons, but it does more than that. I was inspired by the demos that circulated in the 90s and tried to implement some effects. We have plasma, scrolling text, and shaking text—just the classics. Unfortunately, the GPU creates some artifacts in the shader at times, but the effect ended up looking cool.

TODO

Now the apps start getting more serious. This is a list where you can mark items as completed; I called it TODO. You can write your shopping list or daily chores. Since we don’t have keyboard inputs, we have a virtual keyboard on the screen. A future improvement could be allowing the user to include items using a computer, but it’s just an idea for now.

Habit

The first app idea I had was a habit tracker. I’m not sure if I liked the result very much, but what matters is that it’s functional. You can create habits, mark them daily, and track your progress over time. I think it’s one of the most useful ones and the kind of thing that’s cool to have on a device like this that you always carry with you.

Weather

This is definitely the most complex app of all and the only one that needs the internet to work. It’s just a weather forecast app, but I needed to connect it to some APIs to get this data. The result was great, and I confess I’ve preferred using it over the app on my phone.

Conclusion

This was just an introduction to the apps I created. I still want to include a calendar app, but I haven’t decided exactly how it should look or what features it will have. It was a good few weeks working on this project, and I learned a lot about how to develop in LÖVE for these retro handhelds; it even made me want to create real games for them. The next step is to trim some remaining edges in the apps and try to include them in the PortMaster catalog. Once that’s done, I want to start bringing some game ideas to these consoles.

I must say I was quite surprised by this engine. I had a prejudice against Lua for being such a simple language, but it’s undeniable how powerful it is: impeccable documentation, built-in physics and networking, real shaders, various community libraries, and even 3D support. It’s no wonder several commercial games use it and it remains relevant even after all this time. The fact that it works so easily with these consoles also pleasantly surprised me. Expect new projects with it in the near future.

entry.title: Goingo Wasm Demo
entry.content:
• Left-Click
    • On Board - Place Stone
    • On Stone - Show group, liberties and enemies
  • Right-Click - Pass turn
entry.summary: Left-Click On Board - Place Stone On Stone - Show group, liberties and enemies Right-Click - Pass turn

entry.title: Devlog #3 - TileMaps and StateMachines
entry.content:
Introduction

It took me long to write this post because I thought there was nothing interesting to comment on the demos that I created these last weeks. I thought I’d write about why I think that and indicate some more useful ways for those who are doing something similar.

Tilemaps

The game we are creating will use tilemaps, this kind of technique is so old that even the Super Nintendo already had specialized hardware to render these maps. There is no shortage of materials on this issue, but if you want a nice introduction you can read this here.

At demo I did two interesting things to note:

First I mapped the cells of our Diffusion Automata with the tilemap spaces, so I could use the simulation to change which tile should be shown in each space.

In addition, I used a .tmx file from Tiled to generate the initial state of the simulation. So this demo ends up showing how to extract tilemaps data and how to use data to change them as well.

StateMachines

Unlike cellular automata, there is no material missing on Portuguese state machines. They are obligatory in any course of Computing, Systems and sometimes even Administration. For this reason I will not indicate any material, you will probably find a lot on Google and can garimpar what makes the most sense to you. If you are lazy, the Wikipedia article is a quick source to get to know the subject.

In demo this machine was used to control the state of each garden. When the farmer creates a new she begins with the state SECA, so he waters that garden she passes to the state MOLHADA. Once in this new state the farmer no longer needs to interact with the garden, every time cycle it will automatically grow, changing from EM_CRESCEMENT_1 to ‘EM_CRESCEMENT_4`.

To demonstrate that this growth time is independent for each garden, every new created time increases. If you interact with a fully grown vegetable garden the farmer can harvest, which brings it back to the state SECA.

I have no commented code for this demo because it is based on a library called stateless, it is not worth explaining how it works because in your case you may be using a different tool. There are hundreds of ways to implement this solution, use the one that makes the most sense in your case.

Conclusion

This post was quick, I didn’t have much to say but wanted to publish right before the end of the week. In the coming week I should not work in any demo or in the game, I will be traveling to solve some issues of my change. But as soon as I come back I must head in the development of new demos. In particular I want to create a UI to navigate the map and interact with the systems, I’m also reading a little about some ‘design patterns’ to help me override these systems, which is essential for everything to work together.

It should take me to my next post, so don’t get scared if you just hear from me in a month or two. Until then, I recommend taking a look at the new links page that I added to the site, so I find something interesting I should put there.

I hope you enjoyed this material and that it was helpful, I am still working on this site and we gave it to help those who are interested in programming practical things and out of the world web. If you have any questions or suggestions, please contact GitHub or e-mail.

entry.title: Farm Demo v0
entry.content:
• W, A, S, D - Move
  • I - Interact
  • ddMouse Wheel - Zoom
entry.summary: W, A, S, D - Move I - Interact ddMouse Wheel - Zoom

entry.title: Diffusion Automata v1
entry.content:
The goal for this Automata is to simulate the diffusion of a liquid on a solid medium, like soil, sand, etc. It was created as a prototype of a underground water diffusion for my game.

As will notice, there isn’t much complexity in this code, that’s the magic of this kind of algorithm, we can use simple rules to create complex behavior. Here, we simulate the interaction between liquids ans solids over time and space using basic arithmetic

Our board will be represented by a 2d array of vectors, this is just for convenience since we can easily group the two relevant values:

  • Humidity
    • The amount of liquid within the cell
  • Impermeability
    • The resistance of the cell to the movement of liquid trough it

The fields Rocks and Rain just represent cells that are totally impermeable or sources of humidity, respectively.

type HumidityBoard struct {
	values      [][]mgl32.Vec2 // [humidity, impermeability]
	Rocks, Rain [][]bool
	hvrX, hvrY  int
}

The logic to determine the humidity of a cell is simples, since the liquid converges to a uniform distribution over time for all the space, we assume that the new value of humidity of a cell is just the mean between it and their neighbors values. For simplicity, we only count 4 neighbors as the diagram below, where v0 is the current cell:

  v3  
v1 v0 v2
  v4  

To include permeability properties, we use a weighted mean where each factor is defined by that cell impermeability. The catch is that we use the inverse(1/value) for neighboring cells, that way we achieve the effect of a highly impermeable cell resisting either losing or gaining humidity. Then we clamp to new value to a max of 1024.

func (ba *HumidityBoard) Update() error {
	// Store the initial state as reference
	m0 := make([][]mgl32.Vec2, len(ba.values))
	copy(m0, ba.values)

	for x, row := range ba.values {
		for y, v0 := range row {
			// Skip the calculations for humidity sources and impermeable cell
			if ba.Rain[x][y] || v0[1] >= (math.MaxFloat32/5)*4 {
				continue
			}

			// Assume that borders are dry and impermeable
			v1 := mgl32.Vec2{0, math.MaxFloat32}
			v2 := mgl32.Vec2{0, math.MaxFloat32}
			v3 := mgl32.Vec2{0, math.MaxFloat32}
			v4 := mgl32.Vec2{0, math.MaxFloat32}

			// Verify if neighbor exists and use their value
			if x > 0 {
				v1 = m0[x-1][y]
			}
			if x < len(m0)-1 {
				v2 = m0[x+1][y]
			}
			if y > 0 {
				v3 = m0[x][y-1]
			}
			if y < len(m0)-1 {
				v4 = m0[x][y+1]
			}

			// Calculate the weighted mean
			r := ((v0[0] * (v0[1])) + (v1[0] / v1[1]) + (v2[0] / v2[1]) + (v3[0] / v3[1]) + (v4[0] / v4[1])) / (v0[1] + (1 / v1[1]) + (1 / v2[1]) + (1 / v3[1]) + (1 / v4[1]))

			// Clamp values to 1024
			if r > 1024 {
				r = 1024
			}

			// Updates board with new humidity value
			ba.values[x][y][0] = r
		}
	}
	return nil
}

The final result is not perfect, there are parameters that are not accounted like velocity and density of the liquid, but it’s sufficient for our porpoises. Another limitation of this method is lack of conservation of mass. As the simulation evolves the humidity spontaneously drops, which is physically impossible.

As stated previous, this is a prototype and limitation as this are not necessarily concerns for games development. Keep in mind that this algorithm is synchronous, but it’s totally possible to paralelize it.

Hope you liked this material and that it was helpful, I’m still working on this website and demos to help people that are interested in programming things beyond the web development niche. If you have any question or suggestion, please contact me through GitHub or e-mail.
entry.summary: The goal for this Automata is to simulate the diffusion of a liquid on a solid medium, like soil, sand, etc. It was created as a prototype of a underground water diffusion for my game.
